mnemonics.title: Stack vs Heap Memory
mnemonics.content: S.C.R.A.P. - Stack: Compiles, Returns automatically, Automatic variables, Points to local data. Heap: Compiles Runtime, Requires manual management, Allocated manually, Points to dynamic data.

mnemonics.title: Rule of Three
mnemonics.content: D.C.A - Destructor, Copy constructor, Assignment operator. Remember: 'Don't Delete Copy Assignment without Destructor'.

mnemonics.title: Memory Leak Prevention
mnemonics.content: A.R.T. - Allocate with new, Release with delete, nullptr afterwards. Always pair new with delete, new[] with delete[].

mnemonics.title: Dynamic Array Deallocation
mnemonics.content: D.A.R.R. - Delete Array: Use Right brackets []. Never forget the brackets when freeing arrays.

acronyms.usage: C++ idiom where resources are acquired in constructor and released in destructor. Essential for automatic memory management.
Provides containers, algorithms, and smart pointers like unique_ptr and shared_ptr for efficient memory management.
Error that occurs when dynamic memory allocation fails because no sufficient memory is available in the heap.
System call used to change the amount of space allocated in the data segment (heap expansion/contraction).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine stack as a vertical stack of plates. New plates (variables) are added on top and automatically removed when function returns. Grows downward in memory.
Think of heap as a scattered parking lot. Each car (memory block) parks wherever there's space. Finding space takes time. Blocks can be anywhere.
Visualize a bathtub with water (memory). The drain (delete) must be opened to let water out. Forgetting to open drain = water overflow (memory leak).
A address signpost pointing to a demolished house. The sign still shows an address but the house (memory) no longer exists.

analogies.analogy: Static is like a fixed-size parking lot (pre-assigned spaces). Dynamic is like street parking where you find any available spot at runtime.
Think of 'new' as checking into a hotel room and 'delete' as checking out. You must check out (delete), or you'll keep paying (memory leak).
A pointer is like a house address written on paper. The paper (pointer) can exist without a house (memory), or point to a demolished house (dangling pointer).
Like a parking lot with cars parked sporadically - even if 10 spots are free, a large truck can't park if spots aren't contiguous.
